one_line_takeaway: Fine-tuning LLMs with reinforcement learning on verifiable (binary correctness) rewards for math/code tasks empirically and theoretically preserves safety-refusal behavior far better than supervised fine-tuning on the same reasoning tasks, because the reward signal's independence from unsafe content bounds how much RLVR can shift a model's safety distribution.
key_finding: Across the paired evaluation, RLVR-fine-tuned models show statistically indistinguishable-from-zero paired harmfulness changes relative to their base models (mean score delta -0.019, p=0.971; mean rate delta -0.032, p=1.000, both non-significant/consistent with no degradation), while SFT-fine-tuned reasoning models (s1.1-32B, OpenThinker-32B) show large, highly significant harmfulness increases (mean score delta +0.828, p<0.001; mean rate delta +0.2475, p<0.001). Per-model per-dataset numbers in Table 3 (DeBERTa score / HarmBench rate) illustrate the pattern concretely, e.g. on I-BeaverTails: Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct base 1.448/0.143 vs its RLVR fine-tune CodeR1-7B 1.195/0.149 (roughly flat), and Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct base 1.091/0.099 vs Ours-RLVR-32B 1.348/0.157 (small increase) versus Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct base vs the SFT models OpenThinker-32B 1.767/0.275 and s1.1-32B 2.277/0.503 (large increases). Ablations show only marginal safety-drift differences from RL algorithm choice (GRPO vs REINFORCE++), model scale (7B vs 32B), and task domain (math vs code), consistent with the theoretical prediction that drift is bounded by covariance between the safety indicator and the reward signal rather than by these training-configuration choices. Reasoning benchmarks (Table 5) confirm RLVR fine-tuning does not sacrifice capability: e.g. Ours-RLVR-32B improves GSM8K 95.5->95.6, MATH500 81.6->82.2, AIME24 13.3->16.7 over its Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct base.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is: (1) a theoretical framework for RLVR (reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards) safety that models LLM generation as a two-stage process (sample a discrete 'token path' r, then sample tokens conditioned on it), under an assumption that success and safety probabilities are conditionally deterministic given the path (Assumption 1). Building on this, the authors derive the closed-form optimal RLVR policy under KL-constrained reward maximization (Theorem 1: an exponential tilt of the reference policy by success probability), then prove a safety-drift upper bound (Theorem 2) showing the change in expected safety score is bounded by the normalized covariance between the safety indicator and the reward-derived tilting weight. From this they derive a zero-drift corollary under a statistical-independence assumption (Proposition 1: if success and safety are independent given the token path, RLVR exactly preserves the reference model's safety expectation) and a worst-case bound via chi-squared divergence when independence fails (Proposition 2). (2) Empirically, the authors fine-tune Qwen2.5-7B/32B and Qwen2.5-Math-7B variants with RLVR (GRPO and REINFORCE++) on GSM8K/MATH training data, and separately evaluate off-the-shelf RLVR checkpoints (SimpleRL-Zoo, CodeR1) and SFT reasoning models (s1.1-32B, OpenThinker-32B), using a paired pre/post-fine-tuning design with paired t-tests (continuous DeBERTa harmfulness score) and Newcombe's paired-binomial-proportion test (binary HarmBench harmfulness rate) across five safety benchmarks. They report that RLVR fine-tuning produces a paired mean harmfulness-score change of -0.019 (95% CI [-0.040, 0.006], p=0.971) and paired mean harmfulness-rate change of -0.032 (95% CI [-0.041,-0.023], p=1.000) relative to base models -- i.e., no significant safety degradation -- whereas SFT (s1.1-32B, OpenThinker-32B vs Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct) shows a paired mean harmfulness-score increase of 0.828 (95% CI [0.784, 0.871], p<0.001) and paired mean harmfulness-rate increase of 0.2475 (95% CI [0.227, 0.267], p<0.001). (3) Ablations across RL algorithm (GRPO vs REINFORCE++), model scale (7B vs 32B), and task domain (math vs code) showing the safety drift stays small in all cases, plus supplementary checks of decoding temperature and prompt template.
threat_model: This is a safety-alignment/robustness paper, not an attacker-vs-defender adversarial security paper in the classic sense: there is no adaptive attacker crafting inputs to evade a specific defense. The 'threat' being measured is safety degradation as a side effect of benign fine-tuning (capabilities training on math/code data inadvertently eroding refusal/harmlessness behavior) -- an alignment-drift/regression threat model, evaluated using adversarially-curated harmful-instruction prompt sets (I-CoNa hate speech, I-Controversial, I-PhysicalSafety, Q-Harm, I-BeaverTails) that were built by prior work (Bianchi et al. 2024; Ji et al. 2023) specifically to elicit harmful completions. The paper does not introduce a new attack, jailbreak, or adversarial-example method; it evaluates whether a training methodology (RLVR) preserves resistance to those pre-existing harmful prompts compared to SFT.
limitations: The theoretical guarantees (Proposition 1's exact safety invariance) rest on two strong, unverified modeling assumptions: (1) Assumption 1 that success and safety probabilities are conditionally deterministic given a discrete 'token path' r -- an abstraction of the true continuous, high-dimensional generation process that the paper does not empirically validate; (2) Assumption 2, statistical independence between the success function g_x(r) and safety function s_x(r), which the paper explicitly acknowledges can fail ('poorly crafted rewards or unexpected model behavior'), in which case only the weaker chi-squared worst-case bound (Proposition 2) applies, and the paper never estimates this chi-squared term or the covariance in Theorem 2 empirically -- so the tightness/relevance of the bounds to real models is unverified. Training data for the authors' own RLVR models is narrow (GSM8K/MATH only), and Table 5 shows Ours-RLVR-7B-REINFORCE actually regresses substantially on GSM8K (91.4->66.2) and MATH500 (77.2->57.8) even while AIME24 improves, an internal inconsistency in the capability claim ('maintains or modestly improves reasoning') that is not discussed. The paper's own reasoning-capability table also shows RLVR models trail SFT models (OpenThinker-32B, s1.1-32B) by large margins on MATH500 and especially AIME24 (e.g., 16.7% vs 60.0%/53.3%), meaning the safety-preserving RLVR approach may not be capability-competitive with the safety-degrading SFT approach for hard reasoning tasks -- a tradeoff the paper does not foreground. Harmfulness is measured only by two automated classifiers (DeBERTa score predictor, HarmBench), with no human evaluation, and the evaluation prompt is deliberately unguarded/minimal, which may not generalize to more sophisticated multi-turn or adversarially-optimized jailbreak attacks (the datasets used are single-turn instruction sets, not adaptive red-teaming). The 'Ours' models are trained by the same team evaluating them, and comparisons mix models with different training data/provenance (e.g., some base models' own training data is listed as 'Private', limiting reproducibility and apples-to-apples comparison).

datasets_used: GSM8K (training and evaluation, Cobbe et al. 2021)
MATH training dataset levels 1-5 (for the authors' 32B RLVR training) and MATH500 (evaluation, Hendrycks et al. 2021)
I-CoNa (n=178, hate speech instructions, Bianchi et al. 2024 curation of Fanton et al. 2021)
Q-Harm (n=100, drawn from Bai et al. 2022 HH dataset)
quantitative_results: Paired harmfulness score change, RLVR vs base: mean -0.019, 95% CI [-0.040, 0.006], p=0.971 (not significant)
Paired harmfulness rate change, RLVR vs base: mean -0.032, 95% CI [-0.041, -0.023], p=1.000 (not significant)
Paired harmfulness score change, SFT vs base: mean +0.828, 95% CI [0.784, 0.871], p<0.001
Paired harmfulness rate change, SFT vs base: mean +0.2475, 95% CI [0.227, 0.267], p<0.001
Reasoning accuracy, Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct base vs Ours-RLVR-32B: GSM8K 95.5%->95.6%, MATH500 81.6%->82.2%, AIME24 13.3%->16.7%
Reasoning accuracy, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct base vs Ours-RLVR-7B: GSM8K 91.4%->90.4%, MATH500 77.2%->76.2%, AIME24 10.0%->16.7%
Reasoning accuracy, Ours-RLVR-7B-REINFORCE vs Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct base: GSM8K dropped 91.4%->66.2%, MATH500 dropped 77.2%->57.8%, AIME24 rose 10.0%->20.0%
SFT models (OpenThinker-32B, s1.1-32B) substantially outperform RLVR models on AIME24 (60.0% and 53.3% vs 16.7% for Ours-RLVR-32B)
baselines_compared: Qwen2.5-7B-SimpleRL-Zoo and Qwen2.5-Math-7B-SimpleRL-Zoo (external RLVR/GRPO models, Zeng et al. 2025)
CodeR1-7B (external RLVR/GRPO code model, Liu and Zhang 2025)
s1.1-32B (SFT reasoning model, Muennighoff et al. 2025)
OpenThinker-32B (SFT reasoning model, Guha et al. 2025)
